title: Butter Chicken
description: A rich, creamy North Indian curry with fall-apart tender chicken in a velvety spiced tomato-butter sauce. The one we make when we want something that feels like a warm hug.

steps.text: Mix yogurt, lemon juice, turmeric, 1 tsp chilli powder, 1 tsp garam masala, and 1 tsp salt in a bowl. Add chicken, coat well, and marinate for at least 30 minutes (overnight if you can).
Grill or pan-fry the marinated chicken on high heat until charred in spots, about 4–5 minutes per side. Don't worry about cooking through — it finishes in the sauce. Set aside.
In a large heavy pan, melt butter with oil over medium heat. Add onions and cook, stirring occasionally, until deep golden — about 15 minutes. Don't rush this.
Add garlic and ginger, cook 2 minutes until fragrant. Add remaining chilli powder and garam masala, stir for 30 seconds.
Pour in tomato passata. Simmer on medium-low for 15 minutes, stirring occasionally, until oil separates and sauce deepens in colour.
Let the sauce cool slightly, then blend until silky smooth. Return to the pan.
Add the chicken pieces into the sauce. Pour in the cream and stir. Simmer on low for 10 minutes until chicken is fully cooked through.
Crush kasuri methi between your palms and add along with sugar. Taste and adjust salt. Serve with naan or basmati rice.

notes.arnav: Add a tablespoon of cashew paste to make it even creamier. The charring on the chicken is non-negotiable.
notes.ayushi: I like to add a tiny pinch of cardamom powder at the very end. Transforms it.

cookedLog.quickNote: Made for family dinner — big hit!

title: Mango Lassi
description: Thick, cold, and intensely mango-flavoured. A 5-minute drink that tastes like a dessert. Use Alphonso mangoes when they're in season — nothing else comes close.

steps.text: Blend mango pieces until completely smooth. If using fibrous mango, strain through a fine sieve.
Add yogurt, milk, sugar, cardamom, and ice. Blend again for 30–45 seconds until thick and frothy.
Taste — adjust sugar or cardamom. Add rose water if using.
Pour into tall chilled glasses. Garnish with a pinch of cardamom or a sliver of mango.

notes.arnav: For a thicker shake-like consistency, skip the milk and add more yogurt.
notes.ayushi: Frozen mango chunks work great when fresh mangoes aren't available. Add a little less milk.

title: Spaghetti Cacio e Pepe
description: Rome's greatest pasta. Just three ingredients — but the technique is everything. Creamy, glossy, intensely peppery. No cream, no butter shortcuts.

ingredients.notes: grate on the finest side of a box grater

steps.text: Toast peppercorns in a dry skillet over medium heat for 2 minutes until fragrant. Crack coarsely in a mortar or under a heavy pan — you want rubble, not powder.
Mix both cheeses together in a bowl. Add about 3 tbsp cold water and work into a thick paste. Set aside.
Boil pasta in well-salted water. Cook 2 minutes less than package directions — you want it very al dente.
While pasta cooks: toast cracked pepper in the skillet over medium heat for 1 minute. Add a ladle (about 80ml) of pasta water — it will sizzle. Let it reduce slightly.
Drain pasta, reserving 300ml pasta water. Add pasta to the pepper skillet and toss vigorously over low heat, adding pasta water as needed to create steam.

Remove from heat. Let the pan cool for 30 seconds (crucial — too hot and cheese seizes). Add cheese paste and toss rapidly, adding pasta water a splash at a time until you have a creamy, glossy sauce that coats every strand.
Plate immediately. Finish with more cracked pepper and a dusting of Pecorino. Serve at once.

notes.arnav: The off-heat step is everything. I've seized the cheese so many times. Cool the pan and trust the emulsification.
notes.ayushi: I substitute half the Pecorino with Parmesan for a slightly milder flavour.

cookedLog.quickNote: Third attempt — finally got the glossy sauce right!

title: Chocolate Lava Cake
description: Crisp exterior, warm molten centre, rich beyond reason. Deceptively simple once you know the timing. Excellent with a scoop of vanilla ice cream.

steps.text: Preheat oven to 220°C (425°F). Generously butter 4 ramekins (175ml) and dust with cocoa powder. Tap out excess. Refrigerate ramekins while you make the batter.
Melt chocolate and butter together in a heatproof bowl over a saucepan of barely simmering water (bain-marie), stirring until smooth. Remove from heat, let cool for 5 minutes.
In a large bowl, whisk together eggs, egg yolks, and sugar vigorously for 2 minutes until pale and slightly thickened.
Fold the chocolate mixture into the egg mixture with a spatula. Sift in flour and fold until just combined — a few streaks are fine.
Stir in vanilla extract. Divide batter evenly between the prepared ramekins. At this point you can refrigerate them for up to 24 hours.
Bake for 10–12 minutes. The edges should be set and the centre should still have a slight wobble. This is the most important step — do not overbake.
Run a knife around the edge, place a plate on top, and flip confidently. Rest for 30 seconds, then lift the ramekin. Finish with flaky salt. Serve immediately.

notes.arnav: I tested at 11 minutes in our oven and it's perfect. Every oven is different — test once before a dinner party.
notes.ayushi: A spoon of raspberry jam in the centre before baking is a game-changer.

title: Dal Tadka
description: Dhaba-style yellow dal with a crackling ghee-and-spice tadka poured on at the end. Humble ingredients, extraordinary results. Vegan and gluten-free.

steps.text: Wash both dals together until water runs clear. Soak in cold water for 20 minutes if you have time.
Combine drained dal, water, turmeric, and salt in a pot. Bring to a boil, skim any foam, then partially cover and simmer on medium-low for 25–30 minutes until very soft and thick.
Add chopped tomatoes. Mash about half the dal with the back of a spoon for a creamy texture. Simmer 5 more minutes.
Make the tadka: heat ghee in a small pan over high heat. Add cumin seeds — they should crackle immediately. Add dried chillies, then garlic slices. Fry until garlic turns golden.
Add onion to the tadka pan. Cook over high heat for 3 minutes until softened and golden at edges. Add chilli powder and coriander powder, stir for 30 seconds.
Pour the hot tadka directly over the dal (it will sizzle dramatically — that's the point). Stir to combine.
Squeeze in lemon juice, stir in fresh coriander. Taste for salt. Serve with rice or roti.

notes.arnav: The tadka temperature is everything. The ghee must be smoking hot when you pour it in.
notes.ayushi: Add a small piece of asafoetida (hing) to the tadka — about the size of a lentil. Makes the whole thing more aromatic.

cookedLog.quickNote: Weeknight dinner. Fast and incredibly satisfying.

title: Classic Tiramisu
description: The real deal — no shortcuts. Coffee-soaked ladyfingers, pillowy mascarpone cream, bitter cocoa on top. We make this for every dinner party and it always vanishes.

steps.text: Brew a strong batch of espresso and let it cool completely. Add Marsala or rum if using. Pour into a shallow bowl.

Whisk egg yolks and sugar in a heatproof bowl over a pot of barely simmering water (bain-marie). Whisk constantly for 8–10 minutes until the mixture is pale, thick, and holds a ribbon for 10 seconds when the whisk is lifted.
Remove from heat, whisk in mascarpone until completely smooth. Let cool to room temperature.
Whip the cold cream to stiff peaks in a separate bowl. Fold gently into the mascarpone mixture in three additions.
Working quickly, dip each ladyfinger in coffee for exactly 2 seconds per side — don't soak or they'll fall apart. Lay in a single layer in a 23x33cm dish.
Spread half the mascarpone cream evenly over the ladyfingers. Dust with a thin layer of cocoa powder.
Add a second layer of dipped ladyfingers. Top with the remaining cream, smoothing the top.
Cover tightly and refrigerate for at least 4 hours, preferably overnight. Just before serving, dust generously with cocoa powder through a fine sieve.

notes.arnav: The bain-marie step is critical — raw egg yolks won't achieve the same texture or food safety.
notes.ayushi: I add a tiny pinch of salt to the cream — it enhances the flavour dramatically. Also, the longer it sets in the fridge, the better it gets.

cookedLog.quickNote: Dinner party. Made double batch. Still not enough.

title: Masala Chai
description: Strong, spicy, milky chai the way it should be — brewed hard and sweetened properly. Not the watery tourist version.

ingredients.item: loose-leaf Assam tea or strong CTC

steps.text: Lightly crush cardamom pods, cloves, peppercorns, and ginger using a mortar. You want them cracked, not ground.
Bring water to a boil in a small saucepan. Add all the spices, cinnamon, and star anise. Boil hard for 3 minutes.
Add tea and sugar. Boil for another 2 minutes, stirring occasionally. The water should look very dark.
Pour in the milk and bring back to a boil. Watch carefully — it can overflow. Simmer for 2–3 minutes until golden and fragrant.
Strain through a fine sieve into cups. Serve immediately, very hot.

notes.arnav: You must let the tea boil — not just steep. That's what gives it body and colour.
notes.ayushi: I add a small piece of mulethi (liquorice root) for a subtly sweet, complex note.

title: Palak Paneer
description: Creamy spinach gravy with golden-fried paneer cubes. Bright green, deeply flavoured, and nothing like the grey mush you get at most restaurants.

steps.text: Blanch spinach in boiling water for 2 minutes, then immediately transfer to ice water to stop cooking and preserve that brilliant green colour.
Blend the blanched spinach with green chillies until smooth. Set aside.
Shallow-fry paneer cubes in 1 tbsp ghee until golden on all sides. Remove and soak in warm salted water — this keeps them soft.
In the same pan, heat remaining ghee. Add cumin seeds and let them sizzle. Add onion, cook 8 minutes until golden.
Add garlic and ginger, cook 2 minutes. Add tomato and cook until mushy and oil separates, about 5 minutes.
Add turmeric and garam masala, stir for 30 seconds. Pour in the spinach purée and simmer for 5 minutes.
Drain paneer from water and add to the gravy. Add cream, stir, and simmer 3 more minutes. Season with salt.
Serve with a swirl of cream on top. Goes best with butter naan or parathas.

notes.arnav: The ice water bath for the spinach is non-negotiable if you care about the colour.
notes.ayushi: I add a handful of fresh coriander to the spinach before blending — adds freshness.

title: Spaghetti Aglio e Olio
description: Midnight pasta perfection — just garlic, good olive oil, chilli, and pasta water. Ready in 20 minutes. Vegan. Requires no shopping.

ingredients.notes: use good quality — it's the main flavour

steps.text: Bring a large pot of water to a rolling boil. Salt generously — it should taste like mild seawater. Add pasta and cook 2 minutes less than package directions.

While pasta cooks, warm olive oil in a wide pan over medium-low heat. Add garlic slices — they should sizzle gently, not aggressively. Cook for 4–5 minutes until golden and fragrant. Add chilli flakes for the last 30 seconds.
Reserve a large mug (about 250ml) of pasta water before draining.
Add drained pasta to the garlic oil. Pour in about 100ml pasta water and toss vigorously over medium heat. The starchy water and oil will emulsify into a glossy sauce that clings to every strand.
Remove from heat. Add parsley and lemon zest. Toss once more. Serve immediately in warm bowls.

notes.arnav: The pasta water is the sauce — don't skip collecting it. More water = more sauce.
notes.ayushi: I add a handful of breadcrumbs toasted in olive oil on top. Adds incredible texture.

title: Classic Mojito
description: Mint, lime, rum, soda. The most refreshing drink in existence. The secret is bruising the mint gently — you want fragrance, not grass.

steps.text: Add mint leaves and sugar syrup to a highball glass. Muddle gently — press and twist 4–5 times. You want bruised mint that releases oils, not shredded mint that turns bitter.
Add lime juice and rum. Fill the glass with ice.
Top with chilled soda water. Stir briefly with a long spoon — just once or twice to combine.
Garnish with a mint sprig (slap it between your palms first to wake up the aroma) and a lime wheel. Serve immediately.

notes.arnav: Never use a blender or over-muddle. Bitter mojitos are a crime.
notes.ayushi: Virgin version: skip the rum, add a splash of lime cordial. Still delicious.
